digital_id.note: Two distinct layers exist. (1) The physical Resident Identity Card (居民身份证/shenfenzheng), mandatory for citizens 16+, issued from a centralized Ministry of Public Security database, and required by law to open a bank account, register a SIM card, buy train tickets, book hotels, and access most government services — this is the long-standing, de facto mandatory backbone of China's identity system. (2) A newer 'National Cyber ID' (网络身份证) online authentication layer launched 15 July 2025, run by the Ministry of Public Security/Cyberspace Administration; officials describe it as voluntary for individuals but mandatory for platforms to support, and it is already effectively required to post on social media. Both are centralized, state-held systems, not citizen-controlled.

sources.title: Law of the People's Republic of China on Resident Identity Cards
China tightens internet controls with new centralized form of national virtual ID
China: Freedom on the Net 2025 Country Report

cash_payment.note: Cash (RMB) remains legal tender, but Alipay/WeChat Pay dominance created documented friction, especially for elderly people and foreign visitors, prompting repeated regulatory crackdowns. The PBOC has acted against cash-refusing merchants since at least 2018, and a new joint PBOC/NDRC/financial-regulator rule, the 'Regulations on RMB Cash Acceptance and Services,' took effect 1 February 2026, explicitly barring government bodies, institutions and businesses from refusing cash or imposing discriminatory conditions on cash payments, with complaint channels and penalties for violators.

sources.title: China's central bank cracks down on merchants who refuse to take cash
China to enhance rules against businesses that refuse to accept cash from Feb. 2026

mandatory_schooling.note: The 1986 Compulsory Education Law mandates nine years of schooling at a licensed institution. Homeschooling is not explicitly criminalized in statute but sits in a 'gray area' the government has repeatedly closed off: the Ministry of Education (Feb 2019) and State Council (Sept 2017) both publicly affirmed home study is not a legal substitute for compulsory schooling, and schools are legally required to report parents who withdraw school-age children.

sources.title: China - HSLDA homeschooling legal status overview
Home Education in China - International Center for Home Education Research Reviews

social_credit.note: There is no unified national personal 'social credit score.' Merics research finds the system is lowly digitalized, highly fragmented, and mostly targets businesses (e.g. a 2025 update formalized cross-ministry corporate data-sharing via CreditChina). Individual-facing components remain fragmented city-by-city and sector-by-sector — e.g. court blacklists for debt defaulters, transit/fare-evasion blacklists — rather than a single algorithmic citizen score. Commercial products like Ant Financial's Sesame Credit are private, non-mandatory, and distinct from any government system; conflating the two is a common error the popular Western narrative makes.

sources.title: China's social credit score – untangling myth from reality | Merics
China's Social Credit System in 2021: From fragmentation towards integration | Merics

alt_currency.note: Cryptocurrency: banned. The PBOC's 2021 'Notice on Further Preventing and Disposing of the Risk of Virtual Currency Trading' outlawed trading and mining; on 28-29 November 2025 a 13-agency PBOC-led coordination meeting reaffirmed the ban and, for the first time, explicitly classified stablecoins as illegal virtual currency. Gold/precious metals: physical bullion, coins and jewelry purchases remain legal for citizens, but the state increasingly restricts leveraged/paper-gold retail trading (e.g. ICBC ending individual precious-metals trading in 2026) and tightened anti-money-laundering rules for gold/gemstone dealers in mid-2025; gold import requires a PBOC license held by only 13 banks.

sources.title: PBoC emphasises stablecoins are illegal in China
China: Central Bank Issues New Regulatory Document on Cryptocurrency Trading | Library of Congress
China tightens anti-money laundering rules for precious metals and gemstone dealers

mandatory_insurance.note: The PRC Social Insurance Law requires employers and employees to jointly contribute to a broad 'Five Insurances' package: pension, medical, unemployment, work-related injury, and maternity insurance. This applies to virtually all employees, including many foreign workers, and non-compliant employers face escalating fines (up to 3x the overdue amount) and can be sued by employees for statutory severance.

sources.title: China Employee Social Insurance Laws - Harris Sliwoski LLP
China's First Comprehensive Social Insurance Law

travel_restrictions.note: Internal: the hukou (household registration) system has restricted where citizens can access public services (schooling, healthcare, social assistance) since the 1950s; 2024 reforms relaxed some restrictions in wealthy provinces like Zhejiang and Jiangsu, but income/housing requirements still block most rural migrants from full urban public-service access. External: 'exit bans' prevent citizens and some foreigners from leaving China; Safeguard Defenders estimates tens of thousands of people are under exit bans at any time, not counting mass ethnicity-based passport confiscation affecting Uyghurs and Tibetans, and the April 2024 Counter-Espionage Law expanded the legal basis for such bans.

sources.title: CECC Special Topic Paper: China's Household Registration System
Exit bans in China: What are they and why are they causing friction with US? | CNN Business

internet_restrictions.note: Freedom House's Freedom on the Net 2025 report scores China 9/100 (the lowest bracket), citing the Great Firewall's blocking of thousands of domains nationally plus newly documented provincial-level censorship systems (e.g. Henan Province) that block at up to 10x the scale of the national system. Censors removed over a million pieces of content in a single 2025 cleanup campaign per the Cyberspace Administration of China.

sources.title: China: Freedom on the Net 2025 Country Report | Freedom House

free_speech.note: Human Rights Watch's World Report 2025 documents systematic use of vague charges ('picking quarrels and provoking trouble,' 'inciting subversion,' 'sedition') to criminalize speech and activism, citing cases including citizen journalist Zhang Zhan (re-detained after a 4-year sentence for COVID-19 reporting), feminist journalist Huang Xueqin (5-year sentence for #MeToo reporting), and media tycoon Jimmy Lai's ongoing national security trial. Freedom House similarly documents mass removal of online speech critical of the government.

sources.title: World Report 2025: China | Human Rights Watch
